body_txt: So you have an animal in your work that needs a name. Naming them after their own species would be lazy. Calling them what they are in another language could work if there were actually any good-sounding words for it. Naming them after their byproducts would also be cliche. So what to do? Simple, just ask the animal their name , and they'll respond with whatever sound they make. A Dog Named "Woof" is where a work features an animal that's named after the sound they make (for e.g., a cat named Meow -Meow, a duck named Quack ers, or a cow named Moo lissa). Some creativity is often used to add a little more spice to the character's name, such as making it a pun on a human name, but completely straight examples aren't impossible to find. Another thing to note is that media in non-English languages will often have puns on their own onomatopoeia for animal sounds. For example, the sound of a dog barking in Japanese is "wan wan," so when this trope happens in a Japanese work, it'll most likely have that instead of English onomatopoeia. This trope has some overlap with Verbal Tic Name , but this trope applies to characters without a limited vocabulary . Additionally, while Pokémon Speak is the trope where a character's name is the only thing they can say, this trope is the inverse. Sub-Trope of A Lizard Named "Liz" . Not to be confused with Everyone Calls Him "Barkeep" or His Name Really Is "Barkeep" (which is closer to A Dog Named "Dog" than this trope). See also Stock Animal Name and Fur Pattern Naming for other common animal naming conventions.
